highlights: Your body is preparing for ovulation and conception.
Your doctor counts pregnancy starting from the first day of your last period.
healthTip: Start taking a daily prenatal vitamin containing folic acid to support baby's early spinal growth.
checklist: Schedule preconception consultation
Begin taking daily prenatal vitamins
Stop drinking alcohol and limit caffeine

highlights: Ovulation occurs, and fertilization takes place in the fallopian tube.
The fertilized egg divides into a cluster of cells as it travels to your uterus.
healthTip: Focus on high-nutrient foods like leafy greens, whole grains, and lean proteins.
checklist: Track ovulation cycles
Stay hydrated (aim for 8+ glasses of water)
Review safe medications with your provider

highlights: The blastocyst implants into the blood-rich uterine lining.
The placenta starts producing hormones that indicate pregnancy.
healthTip: Stay active with gentle routines like walking, and maintain regular sleep cycles.
checklist: Research local prenatal care providers
Avoid raw foods, soft cheeses, and undercooked meats
Keep up gentle daily exercises

highlights: The embryo splits into the amniotic sac, yolk sac, and baby-to-be.
Early blood vessels are forming to create the heart and circulatory system.
healthTip: Listen to your body. Fatigue is completely normal right now as hormones adjust.
checklist: Take a home pregnancy test if your period is late
Share the news with your partner
Book your first prenatal appointment (usually around week 8)

highlights: The baby's neural tube (which becomes the brain and spinal cord) closes.
The tiny heart starts beating and pumping early blood cells.
healthTip: Stay hydrated and try small, frequent meals if morning sickness begins to show up.
checklist: Keep track of morning sickness triggers
Avoid exhaust fumes, paint fumes, and harsh chemicals
Confirm date of first prenatal appointment

highlights: Facial features like ears, nose, and jaw are beginning to form.
Tiny buds that will grow into arms and legs appear.
healthTip: Eat light, bland snacks like crackers or toast to settle a sensitive stomach.
checklist: Buy comfortable, breathable cotton underwear
Research options for pregnancy journals
Stay hydrated throughout the day

highlights: Baby's brain cells are developing at an incredible rate of 100,000 cells per minute.
Tiny kidneys are in place and will soon start producing urine.
healthTip: Include calcium-rich foods like yogurt, tofu, or fortified plant milks in your daily meals.
checklist: Check ingredients in your skincare products for pregnancy-safe alternatives
Avoid heavy lifting and strenuous tasks
Rest whenever you feel fatigued

highlights: Baby's hands and feet have webbed fingers and toes that are beginning to separate.
The lungs are beginning to form branches of bronchial tubes.
healthTip: Gentle daily walks can help boost your energy levels and improve sleep quality.
checklist: Attend your first prenatal wellness visit
Start a pregnancy journal or milestone photo tracker
Ask your doctor about safe over-the-counter remedies

highlights: The baby's tail structure has completely disappeared, and they are now officially a fetus.
Muscles are starting to develop, allowing tiny, involuntary movements.
healthTip: Make sure you are getting enough iron through spinach, lentils, or lean meats.
checklist: Look into dental checkup options (dental health is extra important now)
Plan comfortable outfits with loose waistbands
Stay active with light stretching

highlights: Baby's bones are beginning to ossify, and teeth buds are forming under the gums.
Vital organs like kidneys, intestines, and brain are functioning.
healthTip: Hydrate! Drinking water helps produce amniotic fluid and supports increased blood volume.
checklist: Look up local prenatal yoga or exercise classes
Begin applying gentle body lotions to keep skin moisturized
Choose a healthy snack routine

highlights: Baby is moving and stretching active limbs, though it is too early for you to feel it.
Fingernails and toenails are starting to grow.
healthTip: Snack on almonds or fresh fruit to combat mid-day energy dips.
checklist: Look into genetic screening tests if recommended by your doctor
Invest in a supportive maternity bra
Drink plenty of fluids

highlights: Reflexes are developing; baby can open and close hands and curl toes.
The vocal cords are starting to form.
healthTip: Eat high-fiber foods to support healthy digestion and keep your energy stable.
checklist: Celebrate reaching the end of the first trimester!
Plan a special announcement for close family and friends
Keep taking prenatal vitamins daily

highlights: The baby's vocal cords are fully developed, and fingerprints are formed.
Intestines move from the umbilical cord into the baby's abdomen.
healthTip: Your energy might start returning! Enjoy this second trimester boost with light exercises.
checklist: Start side-sleeping habits using pillows for support
Research nursery styles and colors
Review your budget for baby items

highlights: Baby can make early facial expressions, including squinting and frowning.
Fine hair called lanugo covers the body for warmth.
healthTip: Keep doing pelvic floor exercises (Kegels) to prepare your muscles for delivery.
checklist: Research local parenting classes or child care options
Hydrate well to avoid dry skin issues
Eat vitamin C rich foods

highlights: The skeleton is continuing to develop, and baby can sense early light changes.
Baby's legs are growing longer than their arms now.
healthTip: Vary your posture throughout the day to prevent lower back pain.
checklist: Talk to your partner about nursery ideas
Take weekly belly progression photos
Attend your regular checkup

highlights: Baby's eyes and ears have reached their final positions on the head.
You might start feeling tiny movements, often described as 'flutters'.
healthTip: If you feel pelvic pain, try sleeping with a pillow between your knees.
checklist: Book the anatomy ultrasound scan (usually at weeks 18-20)
Look up local baby registries
Buy comfortable slip-on shoes

highlights: Adipose tissue (fat) is beginning to develop under the skin to keep baby warm.
The umbilical cord is growing stronger and thicker.
healthTip: Support your lower back by avoiding standing for long periods without breaks.
checklist: Explore names for the baby
Moisturize your stretching belly skin
Attend follow-up doctor appointments

highlights: The baby's ears can now hear internal sounds like your heartbeat and digestion.
Myelin is forming around nerve fibers to speed up brain signals.
healthTip: Sing or speak to your belly! Baby is starting to recognize your voice.
checklist: Prepare questions for the mid-pregnancy ultrasound
Plan a relaxing weekend with your partner
Eat foods rich in Vitamin D

highlights: A protective greasy coating called vernix caseosa covers baby's delicate skin.
Early sensory areas for taste, smell, hearing, and vision are developing.
healthTip: Choose loose-fitting clothes made from natural, breathable fibers.
checklist: Review pediatricians in your local area
Prepare the baby's sleeping space
Maintain active, healthy eating patterns

highlights: Baby's digestive system is now producing meconium (the first stool).
You have reached the official halfway point of your pregnancy journey!
healthTip: Celebrate reaching 20 weeks! Take some time to relax and pamper yourself.
checklist: Complete the anatomy scan ultrasound
Find out the baby's gender if you choose to
Plan a special celebration date

highlights: Baby's heartbeat is now strong enough to be heard with a simple stethoscope.
The bone marrow is taking over blood cell production.
healthTip: Include gentle stretching in your bedtime routine to prevent leg cramps.
checklist: Register for local hospital tours
Finalize baby registry checklist
Stay hydrated before sleeping

highlights: The baby's lips, eyelids, and eyebrows are becoming more defined.
The pancreas is developing rapidly to produce hormones.
healthTip: Keep meals balanced with lean proteins and high-fiber grains.
checklist: Start planning the nursery decoration details
Buy standard baby basic clothing items
Keep active with prenatal routines

highlights: Baby's inner ear is fully developed, establishing their sense of balance.
Lungs are developing surfactant to help them expand after birth.
healthTip: Take warm baths (not hot) to soothe aching joints and muscles.
checklist: Read about post-partum recovery resources
Choose baby names shortlist
Attend regular prenatal checkup visits

highlights: Baby's skin is still translucent but starting to fill out as fat builds up.
Taste buds are fully formed and functioning.
healthTip: Be prepared for the glucose test (checking for gestational diabetes) around this time.
checklist: Schedule the gestational diabetes screening test
Verify maternity leave details with your employer
Eat regular, balanced meals

highlights: Baby's nostrils are starting to open, and they are practicing breathing movements.
Capillaries are forming under the skin, giving it a healthy pink glow.
healthTip: If you experience heartburn, try eating smaller meals more frequently.
checklist: Purchase a car seat and practice installing it
Plan basic safety proofing in your home
Drink plenty of water

highlights: Baby's eyes are starting to open and can blink in response to light.
Brain wave activity for hearing and vision is active.
healthTip: Maintain side-sleeping habits to support healthy blood flow.
checklist: Attend prenatal birth classes
Write a draft of your preferred birth plan
Keep up regular prenatal checks

highlights: Baby is sleeping and waking at regular intervals.
Lungs are now capable of breathing air with medical support.
healthTip: Celebrate the final week of your second trimester! You are doing great.
checklist: Order a breast pump if you plan to pump
Check pediatricians and select one
Prepare comfortable pillows for bed

highlights: Welcome to the third trimester! Baby is growing rapidly from now on.
Eyelashes have formed, and the brain is developing deep folds.
healthTip: Eat calcium-rich foods to help baby build strong bones.
checklist: Ask your doctor about kick count tracking instructions
Pack a diaper bag with emergency supplies
Attend third-trimester prenatal appointments

highlights: Baby's head is growing larger to accommodate their expanding brain.
Muscles and lungs continue to mature daily.
healthTip: Wear comfortable support bands if you feel pressure on your hips.
checklist: Prepare post-partum care essentials
Purchase crib sheets and mattress protectors
Keep track of daily fetal movements

highlights: Baby is shedding lanugo (fine body hair) as body fat increases.
The bone marrow is now fully responsible for producing red blood cells.
healthTip: Rest as much as possible; carrying the extra weight is hard work.
checklist: Pack your hospital bag for labor and delivery
Wash baby clothes and blankets in baby-safe detergent
Drink water to reduce swelling in ankles

highlights: Baby's brain is processing information, signals, and light changes.
The reproductive organs continue to mature.
healthTip: Try gentle prenatal massages to relieve tension in your neck and shoulders.
checklist: Install the baby car seat permanently
Plan the route to the birth center or hospital
Prepare emergency phone numbers list

highlights: Baby is putting on weight quickly, about half a pound per week.
All five senses are active and fully developed.
healthTip: Focus on nutrient-dense meals to support late-stage baby development.
checklist: Stock your freezer with pre-made meals
Attend bi-weekly checkups with your doctor
Practice deep breathing exercises

highlights: Baby's skull bones are soft and flexible to ease passage through the birth canal.
The immune system is receiving antibodies from the mother.
healthTip: Keep active with very light walking; don't push yourself too hard.
checklist: Review your hospital bag items
Prepare baby feeding supplies
Verify your birth plan with your doctor

highlights: Baby's central nervous system and lungs are fully matured.
They are spending most of their time sleeping and growing.
healthTip: Elevate your feet whenever sitting down to keep swelling under control.
checklist: Buy diapers and baby wipes in various sizes
Confirm who will take care of pets/siblings during birth
Set up baby changing station

highlights: Baby is getting quite snug in the uterus, so movements may feel like rolls instead of kicks.
Kidneys are fully developed and processing waste.
healthTip: Take time to relax and read a book or watch a movie; rest is precious now.
checklist: Have Group B Strep (GBS) screening completed
Finalize nursery setup
Rest when fatigue hits

highlights: Baby may drop down into your pelvis (lightening), making it easier to breathe.
The skin is becoming smooth and plump.
healthTip: Practice gentle pelvic tilts to help baby move into the head-down position.
checklist: Start weekly prenatal wellness appointments
Verify pediatric check details
Review labor warning signs

highlights: Your pregnancy is now considered 'early term'! Baby is ready for the world.
Coordination skills are fully developed; baby can grasp fingers.
healthTip: Keep your hospital bag in the car or near the front door.
checklist: Ensure all baby gear is clean and assembled
Double-check car seat installation safety
Rest and relax as much as possible

highlights: Baby is producing surfactant to prepare their lungs for breathing air.
Organ systems are fully functional and ready.
healthTip: Spend quality time relaxing with your partner or family.
checklist: Practice relaxation techniques for labor
Keep track of contraction signals
Eat healthy, light snacks

highlights: Your baby is now 'full term'! They are the size they will be at birth.
The placenta continues to supply nutrients and antibodies.
healthTip: Stay close to home and keep your phone fully charged.
checklist: Review birth breathing techniques
Keep hydrated and take warm showers
Enjoy these final quiet moments

highlights: Your official due date is here! The baby is fully formed and ready to meet you.
Only about 5% of babies are born exactly on their due date, so do not worry if they are late.
healthTip: You are doing amazing! Trust your body and take deep breaths.
checklist: Contact your care provider if contractions start
Rest, nap, and keep energy reserves high
Get ready to welcome your little one! ❤️
